Notably, the left popliteal pulse was very prominent, with a visible pulsatile mass in the left popliteal fossa. Lower extremity edema 1+ was noted from the patient’s left ankle to his knee. The patient was neurologically intact at the time of presentation.

retrieved_docs.title: Ballard and Dubowitz Neonatal Assessments for Gestational Age Determination -- Summary / Explanation -- Dubowitz Scoring System
retrieved_docs.content: In 1970, pediatrician Lilly Dubowitz and neurologist Victor Dubowitz collaborated to develop the Dubowitz scoring system to estimate gestational age in newborns. This scoring system derives from a study conducted in the NICU at Jessop Hospital for Women, Sheffield, England, with a sample size of 167 neonates. [7] The assessment involves 21 criteria encompassing 11 physical and 10 neuromuscular characteristics. Physical criteria include skin color, texture, and opacity; ear form and firmness; plantar creases; edema; lanugo; breast size; and nipple and genital development. Physical criteria are scored on a scale from 0 to 4. Neuromuscular features include posture, ventral suspension, scarf sign, head lag, square window, popliteal angle, ankle dorsiflexion, arm recoil, leg recoil, and heel-to-ear. Neuromuscular criteria are scored on a scale from 0 to 5. The physical and neuromuscular scores are then combined to help determine gestational age. The composite score is plotted on a graph to estimate gestational age between 26 and 44 weeks. Dubowitz et al proposed a regression formula to determine gestational maturity using the total composite score obtained from assessments. The regression formula is y =0.2642 x +24.595, where x represents the total score, and y represents the gestational age. The system uses the LMP as the reference standard, with a reported accuracy of ±2.0 weeks within a 95% confidence interval. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Intoeing -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Physical examination should include inspecting the entire lower extremity, with particular attention to the hip/femur, the knee/tibia, and the feet. The patient's gait should be assessed while walking and while running, as age and developmentally appropriate.

retrieved_docs.title: Intoeing -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: In assessing patients with in-toeing, it is important to remember the age of the patient and the natural course of the lower leg development. The physiologic conditions causing in-toeing will have a spontaneous resolution, requiring good follow up, and parental reassurance. Rare causes will persist past the age of normal correction, but patients do not typically develop sequelae or symptoms. Detailed examination of the feet, ankles, knees, and hips will allow the examiner to differentiate between in-toeing of the possible areas that may be affected.

retrieved_docs.content: The patient lies prone on the examination couch with the hips extended and the hands underneath the forehead. The arms are perpendicular to the body. The patient is then requested to lift both the legs off the couch until the knee is cleared off the couch. The patient should maintain normal breathing during the entire test procedure. The examiner can monitor the knee clearance by sliding a hand under the knee. The clinician records the maximum time that the patient can hold this position. Normative values: Men 38 +/- 6. retrieved_docs.title: Case Study: 86-Year-Old Male With Leg Swelling After Hip Surgery (Archived) -- Discussion
retrieved_docs.content: Endovascular stent-graft repair is a minimally invasive option, which can be used if the anatomy is suitable.  This is determined with CT Angiogram measurements to ensure the existence of an adequate purchase of the graft into normal arterial tissue 2 cm above and below the aneurysm sac. This approach is particularly useful for elderly or higher-risk patients and less durable for younger, more active patients. Endovascular stent-graft repair affords the advantage of shorter postoperative hospital stays compared to open surgery. There are, however, disadvantages, including decreased primary patency (the time the revascularized region remains open without reintervention) and the need for reintervention to accomplish similar secondary patency (the time the revascularized region remains patent with intervention after occlusion). Other considerations include lower perioperative morbidity and faster recovery.  This less invasive therapy lacks long-term follow-up, carries the risk of thrombosis with hyperacute knee flexion, and has limited information regarding long-term stent durability.

retrieved_docs.title: Blount Disease -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Distinguishing infantile tibia vara from physiological bowing of the legs can be challenging. Physiological bowing is characterized by the gradual curve development of both the tibia and femur, whereas proximal tibial bowing in Blount disease is acute. An MDA >11° is indicative of Blount disease in affected patients.

retrieved_docs.title: Case Study: 86-Year-Old Male With Leg Swelling After Hip Surgery (Archived) -- Case Presentation
retrieved_docs.content: An 86-year-old male was noted to have left lower extremity swelling after an uneventful left hip surgery performed for a fracture after a fall down a stairwell two months prior. His other medical co-morbidities include hypertension, gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD), and hyperlipidemia. He denied shortness of breath, prior leg swelling, pain at rest, or foot discoloration.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 4 -- Community 2906
retrieved_docs.content: Amputation of lower extremity is a surgical procedure that encompasses distinct child operations, specifically بتر تحت الركبة (below‑knee amputation) and بتر فوق الركبة (above‑knee amputation). These two levels of amputation represent different anatomical sites of limb removal, each influencing postoperative function, prosthetic fitting, and rehabilitation strategies for the patient.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 755
retrieved_docs.content: The category “THIGH AND LEG: SURGICAL REPAIRS, CLOSURES AND RECONSTRUCTIONS” is a subcategory of “treatment; fracture or dislocation of lower extremity (other than hip or femur)” and encompasses a range of operative interventions focused on the femur, proximal tibia, fibula, and trochanteric region. Specific procedures classified as children of this category include “Arrest, epiphyseal, any method (eg, epiphysiodesis); distal femur,” which addresses growth‑plate modulation of the distal femur, and “Arrest, hemiepiphyseal, distal femur or proximal tibia or fibula (eg, genu varus or valgus),” which corrects angular deformities of the lower limb by partially arresting growth. The category also contains “Internal fixation of femur” and its variant “Internal fixation of femur without fracture reduction,” both of which provide stable fixation for femoral fractures, with the latter used when anatomical reduction is not achieved. Bone‑shaping and lengthening techniques are represented by “Osteoplasty of femur,” “Osteoplasty of femur, lengthening,” and the more complex “Osteoplasty, femur; combined, lengthening and shortening with femoral segment transfer,” which allow for correction of limb length discrepancies and deformities through controlled osteotomy and segment manipulation. Management of post‑traumatic deformity is addressed by “Repair of malunion, of femur, distal to head and neck,” which restores proper alignment of the femur distal to the proximal femoral region. Additionally, “Trochanterplasty” is listed as a child procedure, indicating surgical modification of the greater trochanter for indications such as hip abductor insufficiency or prosthetic fitting. Collectively, these procedures illustrate the comprehensive surgical toolkit available for addressing fractures, dislocations, growth‑plate disorders, angular deformities, malunions, and length discrepancies in the thigh and leg, thereby supporting optimal functional recovery and limb alignment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 1 -- Community 219
retrieved_docs.content: fixation of bone without fracture reduction” is highlighted as a versatile orthopedic technique that stabilizes bones without prior reduction and is applied not only to the femur and hip but also to other lower‑extremity bones, the radius and ulna, and the clavicle. Collectively, these entities illustrate the extensive array of surgical options—ranging from fixation, reduction, and growth arrest to osteoplastic remodeling, prosthetic revision, soft‑tissue repair, and prophylaxis—available for managing structural, functional, and developmental problems of the thigh and leg.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2447
retrieved_docs.content: Hemiplegia and hemiparesis are clinical conditions that fall under the broader category of paralysis; they involve loss of motor function on one side of the body, with hemiplegia denoting complete weakness and hemiparesis indicating partial weakness. Recognizing these disorders as subtypes of paralysis helps clinicians diagnose the extent of neurological impairment and tailor rehabilitation and treatment strategies accordingly.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1508
retrieved_docs.content: Amputation of lower extremity is a broad surgical category that includes several more specific procedures. Within this category, بتر أصبع القدم refers to the amputation of an individual toe, بتر القدم denotes the removal of the entire foot, and بتر فوق الركبة describes an amputation performed above the knee joint. These distinct levels of lower‑extremity amputation differ in the extent of tissue removed, functional impact, and rehabilitation requirements, and recognizing each as a child of the general amputation of lower extremity concept aids clinicians in planning appropriate surgical approaches, postoperative care, and prosthetic fitting for affected patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2283
retrieved_docs.content: Amputation of lower extremity includes several specific procedures: بتر أصبع القدم (toe amputation), بتر القدم (foot amputation), بتر تحت الركبة (below‑knee amputation), and بتر فوق الركبة (above‑knee amputation). These child entities represent the hierarchical subtypes of lower‑extremity amputation, providing a clear framework for clinicians to classify and manage the various levels of limb loss.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1992
retrieved_docs.content: Amputation of lower extremity is a broad surgical category that includes specific procedures such as بتر أصبع القدم (toe amputation) and بتر تحت الركبة (below‑knee amputation). A notable complication associated with these procedures is necrosis of the amputation stump of the right lower limb, which is directly linked to the amputation of lower extremity. This necrotic condition is characterized by the morphological finding النَخَر (necrosis). The relationship between lower‑extremity amputation and subsequent stump necrosis underscores the importance of monitoring for tissue viability and managing potential complications in patients undergoing toe or below‑knee amputations.

retrieved_docs.content: The “THIGH AND LEG: SURGICAL REPAIRS, CLOSURES AND RECONSTRUCTIONS” category encompasses a wide array of operative interventions that address deformities, fractures, malunions, prosthetic complications, and growth‑plate abnormalities of the femur, tibia, fibula, and proximal hip structures.
